cases.rules_text:
1. Use of a prohibited access method is present if any one of the following occurs: a person uses a stolen password, bypasses a login control, or exploits an unpatched service.
2. Protected-system status is present when the system requires authorization and the owner has not permitted the access.
3. Data-copying conduct is present if any one of the following occurs: the person downloads customer records, copies authentication tokens, or exports a database snapshot.
4. Service-disruption conduct is present if any one of the following occurs: the person deletes server files, disables monitoring services, or floods a service with requests.
5. Deceptive payment conduct is present when the person either sends a false payment request or redirects an invoice account.
6. Payment loss is present when a victim payment is actually obtained.
7. Security-testing authorization safeguards are satisfied by a written testing contract, conduct within scope, and prompt reporting.
8. Incident-response authorization safeguards are satisfied by a written response order, containment-limited conduct, and preserved logs.
9. Unauthorized protected-system intrusion is established when prohibited access is made against a protected system.
10. Unauthorized data acquisition is established when protected-system intrusion is combined with data-copying conduct.
11. Unauthorized service interference is established when protected-system intrusion is combined with service-disruption conduct.
12. Completed payment fraud is established when protected-system intrusion, deceptive payment conduct, and payment loss are all present.
13. Security-testing authorization is established when the required security-testing safeguards are satisfied.
14. Incident-response authorization is established when the required response safeguards are satisfied.
15. The unauthorized access article covers unauthorized protected-system intrusion; the prescribed consequence is a fine or short detention.
16. The data theft article covers unauthorized data acquisition; the prescribed consequence is imprisonment of not more than 3 years.
17. The system disruption article covers unauthorized service interference; the prescribed consequence is imprisonment of between 3 and 7 years.
18. The financial cyber fraud article covers completed payment fraud; the prescribed consequence is imprisonment of between 7 and 15 years.
19. The security testing authorization article applies when unauthorized protected-system intrusion and testing authorization are both present; the prescribed consequence is no criminal liability.
20. The incident response authorization article applies when unauthorized protected-system intrusion and incident-response authorization are both present; the prescribed consequence is no criminal liability.
21. Where the data theft article and the unauthorized access article both apply, the data theft article prevails.
22. Where the system disruption article and the unauthorized access article both apply, the system disruption article prevails.
23. Where the financial cyber fraud article and the unauthorized access article both apply, the financial cyber fraud article prevails.
24. Where the financial cyber fraud article and the data theft article both apply, the financial cyber fraud article prevails.
25. Where the system disruption article and the data theft article both apply, the system disruption article prevails.
26. The security testing authorization article operates as an exception to the unauthorized access article when both apply.
27. The security testing authorization article operates as an exception to the data theft article when both apply.
28. The security testing authorization article operates as an exception to the system disruption article when both apply.
29. The incident response authorization article operates as an exception to the unauthorized access article when both apply.
30. The incident response authorization article operates as an exception to the data theft article when both apply.
31. The incident response authorization article operates as an exception to the system disruption article when both apply.
32. The incident response authorization article operates as an exception to the financial cyber fraud article when both apply.

  imports Conflict

and f5: "\<not> owner_permission_for_access actor"

and f15: "\<not> has_security_testing_contract actor"
and f16: "\<not> stays_within_test_scope actor"
and f17: "\<not> reports_findings_promptly actor"
and f18: "\<not> receives_incident_response_order actor"

lemma derive_access_method: "access_method actor"
  using f1
  unfolding access_method_def
  by simp
lemma derive_protected_system: "protected_system actor"
  using f2 f5
  unfolding protected_system_def
  by simp

  unfolding intrusion_basis_def
  by simp

  using derive_intrusion_basis
  by simp


lemma not_trigger_art_data_theft: "\<not> article_triggered Art_Data_Theft actor"
  using f1 f2 f3 f4 f5 f6 f7 f8 f9 f10 f11 f12 f13 f14 f15 f16 f17 f18 f19 f20
  unfolding article_triggered.simps data_acquisition_basis_def intrusion_basis_def access_method_def protected_system_def data_copy_action_def
  by simp
lemma not_trigger_art_system_disruption: "\<not> article_triggered Art_System_Disruption actor"
  using f1 f2 f3 f4 f5 f6 f7 f8 f9 f10 f11 f12 f13 f14 f15 f16 f17 f18 f19 f20
  unfolding article_triggered.simps service_interference_basis_def intrusion_basis_def access_method_def protected_system_def disruptive_action_def
  by simp
lemma not_trigger_art_financial_cyber_fraud: "\<not> article_triggered Art_Financial_Cyber_Fraud actor"
  using f1 f2 f3 f4 f5 f6 f7 f8 f9 f10 f11 f12 f13 f14 f15 f16 f17 f18 f19 f20
  unfolding article_triggered.simps payment_fraud_basis_def intrusion_basis_def access_method_def protected_system_def deceptive_payment_step_def payment_obtained_def
  by simp
lemma not_trigger_art_security_testing_authorization: "\<not> article_triggered Art_Security_Testing_Authorization actor"
  using f1 f2 f3 f4 f5 f6 f7 f8 f9 f10 f11 f12 f13 f14 f15 f16 f17 f18 f19 f20
  unfolding article_triggered.simps intrusion_basis_def access_method_def protected_system_def testing_authorization_def security_testing_safeguards_def
  by simp
lemma not_trigger_art_incident_response_authorization: "\<not> article_triggered Art_Incident_Response_Authorization actor"
  using f1 f2 f3 f4 f5 f6 f7 f8 f9 f10 f11 f12 f13 f14 f15 f16 f17 f18 f19 f20
  unfolding article_triggered.simps intrusion_basis_def access_method_def protected_system_def response_authorization_def incident_response_safeguards_def
  by simp

lemma not_defeated_art_unauthorized_access: "\<not> defeated actor Art_Unauthorized_Access"
proof
  assume defeated_assm: "defeated actor Art_Unauthorized_Access"
  then obtain higher where
    higher_triggered: "article_triggered higher actor"
    and higher_overrides: "overrides higher Art_Unauthorized_Access"
    unfolding defeated_def by blast
  then show False
    using not_trigger_art_data_theft not_trigger_art_system_disruption not_trigger_art_financial_cyber_fraud not_trigger_art_security_testing_authorization not_trigger_art_incident_response_authorization
    unfolding overrides_def
    by (cases higher) auto
qed
lemma applicable_art_unauthorized_access: "applicable actor Art_Unauthorized_Access"
  using trigger_art_unauthorized_access not_defeated_art_unauthorized_access
  unfolding applicable_def
  by blast

  using not_trigger_art_security_testing_authorization
  unfolding applicable_def
  by blast
lemma not_applicable_art_incident_response_authorization: "\<not> applicable actor Art_Incident_Response_Authorization"
  using not_trigger_art_incident_response_authorization
  unfolding applicable_def
  by blast

lemma not_final_no_liability: "\<not> final_no_liability actor"
proof
  assume final_no_liability_assm: "final_no_liability actor"
  then obtain art where
    art_applicable: "applicable actor art"
    and art_result: "article_result art = No_Criminal_Liability"
    unfolding final_no_liability_def by blast
  show False
    using art_applicable art_result not_applicable_art_security_testing_authorization not_applicable_art_incident_response_authorization
    by (cases art) auto
qed
theorem final_result_liability: "final_legal_result_holds actor fine_or_short_detention"
  using applicable_art_unauthorized_access not_final_no_liability
  unfolding final_legal_result_holds_def
  by auto

end

cases.rules_text:
1. Use of a prohibited access method is present if any one of the following occurs: a person uses a stolen password, bypasses a login control, or exploits an unpatched service.
2. Protected-system status is present when the system requires authorization and the owner has not permitted the access.
3. Data-copying conduct is present if any one of the following occurs: the person downloads customer records, copies authentication tokens, or exports a database snapshot.
4. Service-disruption conduct is present if any one of the following occurs: the person deletes server files, disables monitoring services, or floods a service with requests.
5. Deceptive payment conduct is present when the person either sends a false payment request or redirects an invoice account.
6. Payment loss is present when a victim payment is actually obtained.
7. Security-testing authorization safeguards are satisfied by a written testing contract, conduct within scope, and prompt reporting.
8. Incident-response authorization safeguards are satisfied by a written response order, containment-limited conduct, and preserved logs.
9. Unauthorized protected-system intrusion is established when prohibited access is made against a protected system.
10. Unauthorized data acquisition is established when protected-system intrusion is combined with data-copying conduct.
11. Unauthorized service interference is established when protected-system intrusion is combined with service-disruption conduct.
12. Completed payment fraud is established when protected-system intrusion, deceptive payment conduct, and payment loss are all present.
13. Security-testing authorization is established when the required security-testing safeguards are satisfied.
14. Incident-response authorization is established when the required response safeguards are satisfied.
15. The unauthorized access article covers unauthorized protected-system intrusion; the prescribed consequence is a fine or short detention.
16. The data theft article covers unauthorized data acquisition; the prescribed consequence is imprisonment of not more than 3 years.
17. The system disruption article covers unauthorized service interference; the prescribed consequence is imprisonment of between 3 and 7 years.
18. The financial cyber fraud article covers completed payment fraud; the prescribed consequence is imprisonment of between 7 and 15 years.
19. The security testing authorization article applies when unauthorized protected-system intrusion and testing authorization are both present; the prescribed consequence is no criminal liability.
20. The incident response authorization article applies when unauthorized protected-system intrusion and incident-response authorization are both present; the prescribed consequence is no criminal liability.
21. Where the data theft article and the unauthorized access article both apply, the data theft article prevails.
22. Where the system disruption article and the unauthorized access article both apply, the system disruption article prevails.
23. Where the financial cyber fraud article and the unauthorized access article both apply, the financial cyber fraud article prevails.
24. Where the financial cyber fraud article and the data theft article both apply, the financial cyber fraud article prevails.
25. Where the system disruption article and the data theft article both apply, the system disruption article prevails.
26. The security testing authorization article operates as an exception to the unauthorized access article when both apply.
27. The security testing authorization article operates as an exception to the data theft article when both apply.
28. The security testing authorization article operates as an exception to the system disruption article when both apply.
29. The incident response authorization article operates as an exception to the unauthorized access article when both apply.
30. The incident response authorization article operates as an exception to the data theft article when both apply.
31. The incident response authorization article operates as an exception to the system disruption article when both apply.
32. The incident response authorization article operates as an exception to the financial cyber fraud article when both apply.

cases.rules_text:
1. Use of a prohibited access method is present if any one of the following occurs: a person uses a stolen password, bypasses a login control, or exploits an unpatched service.
2. Protected-system status is present when the system requires authorization and the owner has not permitted the access.
3. Data-copying conduct is present if any one of the following occurs: the person downloads customer records, copies authentication tokens, or exports a database snapshot.
4. Service-disruption conduct is present if any one of the following occurs: the person deletes server files, disables monitoring services, or floods a service with requests.
5. Deceptive payment conduct is present when the person either sends a false payment request or redirects an invoice account.
6. Payment loss is present when a victim payment is actually obtained.
7. Security-testing authorization safeguards are satisfied by a written testing contract, conduct within scope, and prompt reporting.
8. Incident-response authorization safeguards are satisfied by a written response order, containment-limited conduct, and preserved logs.
9. Unauthorized protected-system intrusion is established when prohibited access is made against a protected system.
10. Unauthorized data acquisition is established when protected-system intrusion is combined with data-copying conduct.
11. Unauthorized service interference is established when protected-system intrusion is combined with service-disruption conduct.
12. Completed payment fraud is established when protected-system intrusion, deceptive payment conduct, and payment loss are all present.
13. Security-testing authorization is established when the required security-testing safeguards are satisfied.
14. Incident-response authorization is established when the required response safeguards are satisfied.
15. The unauthorized access article covers unauthorized protected-system intrusion; the prescribed consequence is a fine or short detention.
16. The data theft article covers unauthorized data acquisition; the prescribed consequence is imprisonment of not more than 3 years.
17. The system disruption article covers unauthorized service interference; the prescribed consequence is imprisonment of between 3 and 7 years.
18. The financial cyber fraud article covers completed payment fraud; the prescribed consequence is imprisonment of between 7 and 15 years.
19. The security testing authorization article applies when unauthorized protected-system intrusion and testing authorization are both present; the prescribed consequence is no criminal liability.
20. The incident response authorization article applies when unauthorized protected-system intrusion and incident-response authorization are both present; the prescribed consequence is no criminal liability.
21. Where the data theft article and the unauthorized access article both apply, the data theft article prevails.
22. Where the system disruption article and the unauthorized access article both apply, the system disruption article prevails.
23. Where the financial cyber fraud article and the unauthorized access article both apply, the financial cyber fraud article prevails.
24. Where the financial cyber fraud article and the data theft article both apply, the financial cyber fraud article prevails.
25. Where the system disruption article and the data theft article both apply, the system disruption article prevails.
26. The security testing authorization article operates as an exception to the unauthorized access article when both apply.
27. The security testing authorization article operates as an exception to the data theft article when both apply.
28. The security testing authorization article operates as an exception to the system disruption article when both apply.
29. The incident response authorization article operates as an exception to the unauthorized access article when both apply.
30. The incident response authorization article operates as an exception to the data theft article when both apply.
31. The incident response authorization article operates as an exception to the system disruption article when both apply.
32. The incident response authorization article operates as an exception to the financial cyber fraud article when both apply.

cases.rules_text:
1. Use of a prohibited access method is present if any one of the following occurs: a person uses a stolen password, bypasses a login control, or exploits an unpatched service.
2. Protected-system status is present when the system requires authorization and the owner has not permitted the access.
3. Data-copying conduct is present if any one of the following occurs: the person downloads customer records, copies authentication tokens, or exports a database snapshot.
4. Service-disruption conduct is present if any one of the following occurs: the person deletes server files, disables monitoring services, or floods a service with requests.
5. Deceptive payment conduct is present when the person either sends a false payment request or redirects an invoice account.
6. Payment loss is present when a victim payment is actually obtained.
7. Security-testing authorization safeguards are satisfied by a written testing contract, conduct within scope, and prompt reporting.
8. Incident-response authorization safeguards are satisfied by a written response order, containment-limited conduct, and preserved logs.
9. Unauthorized protected-system intrusion is established when prohibited access is made against a protected system.
10. Unauthorized data acquisition is established when protected-system intrusion is combined with data-copying conduct.
11. Unauthorized service interference is established when protected-system intrusion is combined with service-disruption conduct.
12. Completed payment fraud is established when protected-system intrusion, deceptive payment conduct, and payment loss are all present.
13. Security-testing authorization is established when the required security-testing safeguards are satisfied.
14. Incident-response authorization is established when the required response safeguards are satisfied.
15. The unauthorized access article covers unauthorized protected-system intrusion; the prescribed consequence is a fine or short detention.
16. The data theft article covers unauthorized data acquisition; the prescribed consequence is imprisonment of not more than 3 years.
17. The system disruption article covers unauthorized service interference; the prescribed consequence is imprisonment of between 3 and 7 years.
18. The financial cyber fraud article covers completed payment fraud; the prescribed consequence is imprisonment of between 7 and 15 years.
19. The security testing authorization article applies when unauthorized protected-system intrusion and testing authorization are both present; the prescribed consequence is no criminal liability.
20. The incident response authorization article applies when unauthorized protected-system intrusion and incident-response authorization are both present; the prescribed consequence is no criminal liability.
21. Where the data theft article and the unauthorized access article both apply, the data theft article prevails.
22. Where the system disruption article and the unauthorized access article both apply, the system disruption article prevails.
23. Where the financial cyber fraud article and the unauthorized access article both apply, the financial cyber fraud article prevails.
24. Where the financial cyber fraud article and the data theft article both apply, the financial cyber fraud article prevails.
25. Where the system disruption article and the data theft article both apply, the system disruption article prevails.
26. The security testing authorization article operates as an exception to the unauthorized access article when both apply.
27. The security testing authorization article operates as an exception to the data theft article when both apply.
28. The security testing authorization article operates as an exception to the system disruption article when both apply.
29. The incident response authorization article operates as an exception to the unauthorized access article when both apply.
30. The incident response authorization article operates as an exception to the data theft article when both apply.
31. The incident response authorization article operates as an exception to the system disruption article when both apply.
32. The incident response authorization article operates as an exception to the financial cyber fraud article when both apply.
cases.case_text: Riley bypassed the login control. The target system required authorization. Riley disabled a monitoring service. Riley redirected an invoice account. A victim payment was obtained. Riley had a written security-testing contract. Riley stayed within the agreed testing scope. Riley promptly reported the findings. Riley received a written incident-response order. Riley limited the actions to containment. Riley preserved activity logs. The system owner did not permit Riley's access.
